title: Understanding your electricity bill and smart tariffs
subtitle: Unit rates, standing charges, Economy 7, time-of-use, and export payments
excerpt: The standard electricity bill has two components: a standing charge (around 61p/day) and a unit rate (around 27p/kWh at the 2025/26 cap). Smart meters enable time-of-use tariffs that charge different rates by hour, useful if you have a battery, heat pump, or EV. This guide decodes the bill and the tariff options.
sourceNotes: OFGEM Energy Price Cap methodology Q3 2025. OFGEM Smart Export Guarantee register. OFGEM Economy 7 and time-of-use tariff guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: Kilowatt-hour. The unit electricity is billed in. One kilowatt-hour equals one kilowatt of power used for one hour (e.g., a 1 kW electric heater running for one hour).

content.text: Your electricity bill has two distinct charges. The unit rate (p/

) is what you pay for each unit of electricity you use. The standing charge (p/day) is a fixed daily fee regardless of consumption. At the 2025/26 price cap: unit rate 24.5p/kWh, standing charge 61p/day, approximately in standing charges alone before you use anything.

Economy 7 is an older two-rate tariff with a lower overnight rate (usually midnight to 7 am) and a higher daytime rate. Day rate is typically 35-40p/kWh; night rate 12-15p/kWh. It was designed for storage heaters that charge overnight and release heat during the day. For households without storage heaters or overnight EV charging, Economy 7 is usually more expensive overall than a single-rate tariff because the higher daytime rate applies to most consumption.

Smart meters enable half-hourly pricing. Tariffs such as Octopus Agile vary the rate every 30 minutes based on wholesale electricity prices. In periods of high wind generation, rates can drop to 1-5p/kWh or even go negative. Peak demand periods (4-7 pm in winter) can see rates of 40-80p/kWh. A battery or flexible heat pump load can shift consumption from peak to off-peak, saving at current price volatility.

If you have solar PV or other generation, the Smart Export Guarantee (SEG) requires licensed electricity suppliers with 150,000+ customers to offer an export tariff. Rates range from 4p/kWh (minimum) to 15p/kWh for competitive tariffs. You need a smart meter for SEG payments, as the exported units must be measured. Suppliers are not obliged to offer SEG to all generators, check eligibility requirements.

The unit rate and standing charge for your current tariff are on any bill, on your online account, or on the OFGEM register. To compare tariffs: multiply your annual kWh consumption by the unit rate difference (in pence), add the standing charge difference × 365. The result is the annual cost difference between tariffs.
